title: Maximum Product Subarray
description:
Scan through an integer array `nums`, which might contain negatives and zeros, looking for whichever contiguous stretch of elements multiplies together into the biggest possible product, then return that product. This is more subtle than summing: a pair of negative factors cancels out to a positive, so a stretch that currently multiplies to a deeply negative number can flip into the best answer the instant one more negative value joins it, meaning a single running best is not enough to track on its own.

**Example**

```
nums = [2, 3, -2, 4]  ->  6    (the subarray [2, 3])
nums = [-2, 3, -4]    ->  24   (the whole array: -2 * 3 * -4)
```
hints: Because two negative numbers multiply into a positive, track both a running maximum AND a running minimum product ending at each index.
At each element, the new running max/min is the best/worst of: the element alone, the previous running max times the element, or the previous running min times the element.
A zero breaks any run -- both running values collapse to zero and the next subarray effectively restarts from the following element.

solutions.name: Brute-force all subarrays
solutions.explanation: Compute the product of every contiguous subarray directly by extending the end pointer for each start pointer, keeping a running best. Straightforward but quadratic.

solutions.name: Running max and min DP
solutions.explanation: Scan once, maintaining the maximum and minimum product of a subarray ending at the current index. A negative current element can turn the running minimum into the new running maximum, so both must be updated together from the same three candidates: the element alone, max-so-far * element, and min-so-far * element.
solutions.code:
def max_product(nums):
    best = cur_max = cur_min = nums[0]
    for x in nums[1:]:
        candidates = (x, cur_max * x, cur_min * x)
        cur_max = max(candidates)
        cur_min = min(candidates)
        best = max(best, cur_max)
    return best
